title: Drumming Practices for Stuck Anger
dek: *Your anger is not a problem to solve but a pattern to move. Drumming gives stuck emotion a physical channel, translating what your body knows but cannot speak into rhythm, sound, and the honest percussion of hands meeting membrane. Here is how to begin.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Drumming transforms stuck anger into movement by giving your body a rhythmic container for emotions the nervous system cannot process through words alone. Specific patterns and tempos guide rage from your chest and jaw into your hands, releasing the freeze response that traps anger in tissue. Regular practice rewires how your body metabolizes frustration before it calcifies into chronic tension or explosive outbursts.

Most of us have been taught that anger needs to be understood before it can be released. We believe that if we can just name the source, trace the wound, articulate the boundary violation, then the heat will dissipate. But anger does not always live in the part of you that speaks. Sometimes it lives in your jaw, your solar plexus, the place between your shoulder blades where you have been bracing for years. Drumming meets anger where it actually lives, in the body, and offers it a way out that does not require translation into language first. ## Why Stuck Anger Needs Movement, Not More Analysis Gabor Maté has written extensively about how unprocessed emotion becomes trapped in the body's tissues when we lack safe ways to express what we feel. The anger that gets stuck is not the anger you yelled about last Tuesday. It is the anger you swallowed at seven, at fourteen, in all the moments when your nervous system registered threat but your environment punished visibility. That anger does not sit in your prefrontal cortex waiting for insight. It sits in your fascia, your gut, the chronic tension in your neck. Over the years I've seen people spend months in talk therapy circling the same resentments, able to describe every detail of the injury but unable to discharge the charge itself. The body needs a different kind of permission. Drumming offers that. When your hands meet a drum skin in a steady, repetitive pattern, you are giving your nervous system a task it understands at a primal level: rhythmic, repetitive action that discharges energy without requiring words. You do not need to be musical. You do not need a expensive drum. A djembe, a frame drum, even an overturned bucket with a towel stretched across it will work. What matters is the physical act of striking a surface in rhythm, the vibration that travels up your arms and into your chest, the sound that fills the room and confirms that your anger is real, is heard, is moving. ## The Physiology of Rhythm and Rage When anger gets stuck, it often triggers a freeze response. You feel the heat rise, your heart rate spikes, and then nothing. The energy has nowhere to go, so it collapses inward. Your shoulders hunch. Your breathing shallows. You feel both wired and exhausted at the same time. This is your autonomic nervous system caught between fight and shutdown, unable to complete the cycle. Drumming interrupts that freeze. The repetitive motion activates your motor cortex and gives your hands something to do with the energy that was building in your chest. The rhythm entrains your heart rate, gradually slowing it if you drum at a steady tempo, or giving it permission to spike and release if you drum faster. The NIMH page on emotional regulation notes that physical movement is one of the most effective ways to shift autonomic arousal, and drumming combines movement with sound, creating a multisensory feedback loop. In my own practice, I have found that the moment the anger starts to move is often marked by a shift in how the drum sounds. The strikes become less controlled, more visceral. You stop trying to make a pretty rhythm and start letting your hands speak what your throat cannot. Your whole torso might get involved, rocking slightly, breathing harder. This is not loss of control. This is your body finally completing a cycle it has been trying to finish for months or years. ## Starting a Practice: The First Five Minutes You do not need to set aside an hour. You need to show up. Here is a practice you can do right now, in under five minutes, with whatever drum or drum-like object you have. Sit with the drum in your lap or on a low table in front of you. Place both hands flat on the drum skin and take three slow breaths. Feel the surface under your palms. Now begin to strike the drum with alternating hands, slow and steady, like a heartbeat. One-two, one-two. Do not try to be loud. Do not try to be interesting. Just keep the rhythm steady for one minute. After that minute, let your hands speed up slightly. Let the strikes get a little harder. If you feel heat in your chest, let it travel down your arms and into your hands. If your jaw is clenched, open your mouth slightly and breathe through it while you drum. Keep going for two more minutes. If you need to make sound, grunt, sigh, yell, let it come out with the drumming. No one is grading you. At the end of three minutes, slow the rhythm back down to the heartbeat pace. One-two, one-two. Take three more breaths with your hands resting on the drum skin. Notice what you feel. You do not need to feel calm. You might feel more awake, more present, maybe a little shaky. That is the anger moving. ## Tempo, Pattern, and Permission Different rhythms do different things. A slow, steady beat (around 60-80 beats per minute) tends to ground and soothe. It matches a resting heart rate and can help you come down from a spike of rage without collapsing into numbness. A faster beat (100-120 bpm) gives you permission to let the anger peak, to hit harder, to feel the full force of what you have been holding. Soraya Chemaly has written about how women in particular are socialized to suppress anger, to the point where many cannot even recognize it in their own bodies. They feel anxious, sad, tired, but not angry. Drumming can bypass that conditioning. Your hands know how to hit something. Your body knows how to make noise. When you give yourself permission to drum with force, you are giving yourself permission to occupy space, to be loud, to impact your environment. That permission alone can be new. You can experiment with patterns. Try four strong beats followed by four soft ones. Try drumming only with your dominant hand for a while, then switch. Try drumming with your eyes closed. Try drumming while humming or chanting a single vowel sound. The pattern is less important than the consistency. Stuck anger needs repetition. It needs you to show up, again and again, and offer your body the same rhythmic container until the anger learns it is safe to move. Across the articles we've published on this site, we return often to the idea that healing is not a single breakthrough but a series of small, repeated actions. Drumming is one of those actions. You do not drum once and discharge a lifetime of rage. You drum for five minutes, three times a week, and slowly teach your nervous system that anger can move without destroying you or anyone else. ## What Happens in Your Body While You Drum The physical sensations can be intense. You might notice heat rising in your face, a tingling in your hands, a sudden urge to cry or laugh. Your heart rate will likely increase, especially if you are drumming faster or harder. You might feel trembling in your legs, a loosening in your jaw, a release of tension in your shoulders. All of this is your body completing the stress cycle that got interrupted when the anger first arose. Thich Nhat Hanh taught that anger is like a crying baby. You do not ignore it, and you do not punish it. You hold it with awareness and breathe with it until it calms. Drumming is a way of holding your anger with your hands. The rhythm becomes the breath. The sound becomes the witness. You are not pushing the anger away, and you are not indulging it. You are giving it a form, a tempo, a beginning and an end. Sometimes you will finish a drumming session and feel lighter, almost euphoric. Other times you will feel raw, like you peeled back a layer and found more anger underneath. Both are okay. The point is not to feel better immediately. The point is to move what has been stuck. Over time, the anger becomes less dense, less chronic. It starts to flow more easily, to rise and fall in response to actual events rather than simmering constantly in the background. If you are new to working with anger in this way, consider keeping something grounding nearby. A cup of herbal tea can help you transition out of the practice. [Yogi Tea Stress Relief & Relaxation Variety (5 packs)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0735W1XZP?tag=spankyspinola-20) offers a gentle way to signal to your nervous system that the discharge is complete and it is time to rest. You might also find that magnesium supports the muscle tension that often accompanies stuck anger. [Natural Vitality Calm Magnesium Capsules (120 ct)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0BP4TQFC1?tag=spankyspinola-20) can be a helpful daily support if you notice chronic jaw clenching or shoulder tightness. ## Drumming with Others: Circle Work and Collective Release While solo drumming is powerful, drumming in a group has its own medicine. When you drum with others, you are not just moving your own anger. You are contributing to a collective rhythm, a shared container that can hold more than any one person could alone. You do not need to talk about what you are angry about. You just show up, add your rhythm to the circle, and let the sound do the work. Many communities have drum circles that meet weekly or monthly. Some are helped, some are open and improvisational. If you cannot find one near you, consider starting your own. Invite two or three friends. Bring whatever drums or percussion instruments you have. Set a simple intention: we are here to move what is stuck. Then drum together for twenty or thirty minutes. You will be surprised how quickly the group finds a rhythm, how the individual anger of each person blends into something larger and less personal. There is something about hearing your own anger reflected and strengthened by others that makes it less shameful. You realize you are not alone in your rage. You realize your anger is not too much, not wrong, not a sign that you are broken. It is just energy that needs to move, and it moves more easily when it has company. ## Integrating Drumming into a Broader Anger Practice Drumming is not a replacement for therapy, boundary work, or the slow process of learning to speak your anger in relationship. It is a complement. Think of it as one tool in a larger toolkit. Some days you will need to journal. Some days you will need to talk to a friend or therapist. Some days you will need to drum. You might also explore other somatic practices that work well alongside drumming. Breathwork, shaking, dancing, and even certain herbal supports can help your body metabolize anger more efficiently. [Real Mushrooms Reishi Capsules (90 ct)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0B21NBL5T?tag=spankyspinola-20) is traditionally used to calm the spirit and support the body's response to stress, which can be helpful if your anger is chronic and accompanied by insomnia or fatigue. You can learn more about herbal approaches in [our herbal cabinet](/herbs), where we explore plants that support emotional processing without numbing you out. The key is to build a practice that honors both the need to discharge and the need to integrate. Drumming helps you discharge. Stillness helps you integrate. After a drumming session, take a few minutes to sit quietly, to notice what has shifted, to let your nervous system settle. You might write a few sentences in a journal. You might lie down and rest. You might go for a walk. The integration is as important as the release. For more on building a whole-body anger practice, you can explore [more articles in this journal](/articles), where we look at anger through the lenses of somatics, herbalism, breathwork, and relational repair. You will also find companion pieces on [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com) that explore the spiritual dimensions of working with difficult emotions. ## Common Questions About Drumming and Anger ### Can drumming make my anger worse? Drumming can temporarily intensify your anger because it brings it to the surface. This is not the same as making it worse. When anger has been suppressed for a long time, any practice that invites it to move will make it feel bigger at first. The key is to stay with the practice, to drum in short sessions rather than trying to purge everything at once, and to give yourself time to integrate afterward. If you find that drumming consistently leaves you feeling more reactive or destabilized, it might be helpful to work with a somatic therapist who can support you in titrating the release. ### What if I live in an apartment and cannot make noise? You have options. A frame drum or a hand drum can be played quietly by adjusting how hard you strike it. You can also place a towel over the drum skin to muffle the sound. Some people use a practice pad designed for drummers, which is nearly silent. You can even drum on your own body, on your thighs or chest, which will not create the same resonance but will still give your hands something to do and your nervous system a rhythm to follow. The physical act of repetitive striking is more important than the volume. ### How often should I drum to see a difference? Start with three to five minutes, three times a week. Consistency matters more than duration. After a few weeks, you will likely notice that your baseline tension has decreased, that you are less reactive to small irritations, or that you have more access to your anger when you actually need it. Some people drum daily as a maintenance practice. Others drum only when they feel anger building. Listen to your body. If drumming feels like a relief, do more. If it feels like a chore, pull back and revisit it when you are ready. ## The Long Work of Becoming Fluent in Anger Drumming will not make you a person who never gets angry. It will make you a person who knows what to do with anger when it arrives. You will still have days when you want to throw something, when you feel the old heat rising and the old story starting to play. But you will also have a practice, a rhythm, a way to meet that heat with your hands and your breath and your willingness to let it move. The work of becoming fluent in your anger is long work. It is the work of unlearning the idea that anger makes you bad, that rage makes you dangerous, that intensity makes you too much. It is the work of remembering that your body is wise, that your nervous system is trying to protect you, and that the energy you have been calling a problem is actually a resource. Drumming is one way to remember. You do not have to be healed to begin. You do not have to have it all figured out. You just have to be willing to sit with a drum, to place your hands on the skin, and to let your body tell you what it needs to say. The rest will come. The rhythm will teach you. Your hands already know the way. --- *Last updated: January 2025* *Written by The Oracle Lover for [The Anger Practice](/about), an independent journal exploring the intelligence of difficult emotions.* ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 28, 2026. In our work on drumming practices for stuck anger, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
